notes: Hard blind seed set for B2. Cases intentionally include non-1:1 step-rubric structures (extra noisy steps, one-to-many labels, many-to-one labels, non-fixed order mapping).

steps.text: I started expanding everything line by line, then realised only selected terms are needed.
From (3+2ax)^5, the x^3 term comes from choosing three ax factors, so I wrote that coefficient.
For (2+ax)^6, I isolated the x^2 term and simplified its coefficient separately.
The statement says first coefficient equals six times the second, so I formed one equation in a.
After cancellation I obtained a=2 and kept the non-zero value required here.

rubric_points.description: from 3 2ax 5 the x 3 term comes from choosing three
for 2 ax 6 i isolated the x 2 term and simplified
the statement says first coefficient equals six times the second so i
after cancellation i obtained a 2 and kept the non zero value

steps.text: From dy/dx = 1/2 x + 72/x^4, substituting x=2 gave tangent gradient 11/2.
I first wrote slope -11/2 for the normal and then corrected that.
Normal slope is negative reciprocal, so m_n=-2/11.
Through P(2,8), I wrote y-8=(-2/11)(x-2).
Separately, integrating gave y=x^2/4-24/x^3+c and substituting P forced c=10.

steps.text: Each arc has radius 2 and angle 60 degrees, so one arc is 2*(pi/3).
Multiplying by three gave perimeter 2pi.
For area I treated the shape as 3 sectors minus 2 triangles.
That simplifies to 2pi-2root3 in exact form.
I had 2.82 on calculator first, then reverted to exact as instructed.

steps.text: From theta=pi/6, I used cos theta=root3/2 and 2-sin theta=3/2.
Hence a=1/2 and r=root3 for the progression.
I substituted into S10 = a(1-r^10)/(1-r).
After tidying powers, I rationalised the denominator to a clean surd form.
Final exact result was written as 121/(root3-1).

Substituting that into part (c) reduced everything to a solvable cosine equation.
I converted cos values back to theta and kept only 0<theta<180 degrees.
One reflex candidate was crossed out after interval check.

steps.text: I swapped x and y in y=(x+a)^2-a, then took square root to invert.
Because original x<=-a, the inverse must use the negative root branch.
So domain of f^-1 is x>=-a while its output stays <=-a.
For the composite condition I set f(x)=1/2 and built the resulting quadratic.
Two roots came out; only the one satisfying the branch restriction was kept.
I boxed the valid root and struck through the rejected one.

steps.text: I set the two curve equations equal and used u=sqrt(x) to simplify the powers.
That gave intersection x=1 and x=25.
Area is integral of upper minus lower, so I integrated each fractional-power term carefully.
Then I evaluated the antiderivative at both limits and subtracted.
Final area matched the exact form, with 42.7 as a decimal sense-check.

steps.text: For x=(ln t)^2 I found dx/dt = 2ln(t)/t.
For y=e^(2-t^2) I obtained dy/dt = -2t e^(2-t^2).
Hence dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt).
Substituting t=e simplified the gradient to a single exponential term.
I cancelled common factors before the final simplification step.

Using tan(theta)=y/x at that point gave the maximum argument.
